Day 2: Understanding HTTP, HTTPS & How Browsers Actually Talk to Servers
Yesterday, I learned how a browser finds the correct server. Today, I wanted to understand what happens next.
In my previous article, I explored how a browser uses DNS to find the IP address of a website.
That naturally led me to another question:
Once the browser knows where the server is, how do they actually communicate?
The answer is HTTP.
At first, HTTP felt like just another acronym that developers use every day. But once I stopped trying to memorize it and started asking why it exists, everything became much clearer.

🌐 What is HTTP?
HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is simply a communication protocol.
In simple words, it’s a common language that both the client and the server agree to use while talking to each other.
Without HTTP, every browser could communicate differently, and every server would have to understand countless formats.
Instead of chaos, HTTP provides one standard way to communicate.

🔒 Then Why Do We Need HTTPS?
This raised another interesting question.
If I’m sending my login credentials, banking details, or personal information over the internet, shouldn’t that communication be protected?
That’s exactly why HTTPS exists.
HTTPS is simply the secure version of HTTP.
It encrypts the communication between the client and the server, making it significantly harder for anyone intercepting the traffic to understand the transmitted data.
In today’s web, HTTPS isn’t just recommended — it’s expected.

📨 Requests and Responses Finally Made Sense
One analogy completely changed how I looked at this.
Imagine walking into a restaurant.
You place an order.
That’s your request.
The kitchen prepares your food.
That’s the server processing your request.
The waiter brings your food back.
That’s the response.
Every website works in a very similar way.
The client sends a request.
The server processes it.
The server sends back a response.
The client receives that response and decides how to display it.

💡 My Biggest Realization
This was probably the most valuable lesson for me today.
The server doesn’t update the user interface.
It simply returns data.
It’s the frontend application — whether it’s built with React, Angular, Vue, or a mobile framework — that decides what should happen next.
For example, after a successful login, the server doesn’t say:
“Open the Home Screen.”
It simply responds with something like:
“Login Successful.”
The frontend reads that response and decides to navigate the user to the home page.
That small distinction completely changed the way I think about frontend and backend responsibilities.

Understanding HTTP Methods
Another concept that became much easier after thinking about intentions instead of definitions was HTTP methods.
Every request has a purpose.
Sometimes we want to read data.
Sometimes we want to create something new.
Sometimes we want to update existing information.
Sometimes we want to delete it.
That’s why HTTP provides different methods.

• GET → Retrieve existing data.

• POST → Create new data.

• PUT → Update existing data.

• DELETE → Remove existing data.
Instead of memorising them, I started thinking about intent.
For example, in a task management application:

• Viewing all tasks → GET

• Adding a new task → POST

• Editing a task → PUT

• Removing a task → DELETE
Thinking in terms of intention made these methods much easier to remember.

💬 Community Insight
One of the readers of my previous article shared an interesting point that I hadn’t connected before.
DNS lookups don’t necessarily happen every single time we visit a website.
The browser, operating system, router, and DNS resolver can cache DNS information for a period of time. That’s one of the reasons websites often load much faster on subsequent visits.
I really enjoyed this insight because it connected two concepts I’d previously thought of separately: DNS resolution and caching.
That’s exactly why I wanted to document this journey publicly — learning doesn’t stop when the article is published. Sometimes the discussion afterwards becomes just as valuable.

🚀 What’s Next?
The next step in my journey is moving into the technologies I work with every day.
I’ll start by revisiting one of the most fundamental questions in frontend development:
Why does HTML still exist — even in the world of React, Angular, AI, and modern frameworks?
I’m looking forward to connecting the fundamentals with the technologies we use every day.
If you’re also revisiting the basics or strengthening your software engineering fundamentals, I’d love to have you along on this journey.
If you have any questions, suggestions, or feedback, feel free to email me at urbashijrall3003@gmail.com.
